title: Use Unique User Accounts
shortText: Never share logins on CJIS systems; every user must have a unique account.
longText: CJIS requires that organizational users be uniquely identified so that every action on a CJIS-connected system can be tied back to a specific person. Shared or generic accounts destroy accountability and make it nearly impossible to investigate misuse or a breach. Replace generic accounts like 'RecordsUser' or 'Admin1' with named accounts, and review existing accounts for any that are shared.
section: AC-2 Account Management / IA-2 Identification and Authentication (Organizational Users)

title: Disable Unused Accounts Promptly
shortText: Disable CJIS accounts immediately when staff leave or no longer need access.
longText: Account management controls in CJIS 6.0 expect agencies to remove or disable access as soon as it is no longer required. Dormant accounts, especially with elevated privileges, are a prime target for misuse and external attackers. Build a simple off-boarding checklist so that HR, IT, and CJIS Security staff know exactly which accounts to disable when someone transfers or departs.

title: Apply Least Privilege
shortText: Give users only the CJIS permissions they need to do their jobs—nothing more.
longText: Least privilege is a core CJIS principle: users should only have access to the information and functions that support their assigned duties. Broad 'super user' or 'admin' roles for everyday work increase risk and amplify the impact of a compromise. Map roles to actual tasks and regularly review permissions to remove access that is no longer justified.

title: Separate High-Risk Duties
shortText: Avoid giving one person sole control over sensitive CJIS functions.
longText: Separation of duties reduces the chance that one person can perform inappropriate actions without detection. Under CJIS, functions such as system administration, security monitoring, and audit log review should not all rest with a single individual. Use role design and technical controls so that critical steps require at least two people, or at minimum are heavily monitored.

title: Lock Devices When Idle
shortText: Configure CJIS workstations and terminals to lock after a short period of inactivity.
longText: Device lock and session timeout requirements exist to prevent unauthorized viewing or misuse when a user steps away from their desk or mobile terminal. Screen savers with secure re-authentication, or automatic session termination after inactivity, are simple controls that protect CJI from shoulder surfing and walk-up access. Work with IT to set consistent timeouts across desktops, laptops, MDTs, and kiosks.
section: AC-11 Device Lock / AC-12 Session Termination

title: Enforce Strong Authentication for Remote Access
shortText: Require multi-factor authentication for any remote CJIS access.
longText: CJIS 6.0 expects stronger authentication when users access CJIS resources from outside the controlled network. Password-only remote access is not sufficient. Implement multi-factor authentication—ideally using phishing-resistant methods—on VPNs, remote desktops, and web portals that handle CJI. Periodically test remote paths to confirm that no legacy or backdoor access bypasses MFA.
section: AC-17 Remote Access / IA-2 Multi-Factor Authentication

title: Secure Wireless Access to CJI
shortText: Treat wireless networks that can reach CJI as high-risk and secure them accordingly.
longText: Wireless access controls in CJIS 6.0 require strong authentication, encryption, and configuration management when Wi-Fi can reach CJIS services. Use enterprise-grade authentication, restrict which SSIDs can access CJIS networks, and avoid open or shared credentials. Regularly review wireless configurations and logs to ensure only authorized devices are connecting.

title: Restrict Use of External Systems
shortText: Limit when and how staff can move CJI to external or non-CJIS systems.
longText: CJIS requires agencies to control how CJI is accessed from or transferred to systems outside of the CJIS-controlled environment. This includes cloud services, personal devices, and partner systems. Define and document what is allowed, require written agreements where appropriate, and technically block unapproved uploads or syncing of CJIS data to consumer services.

title: Provide Regular Security Awareness Training
shortText: Make sure everyone with CJIS access receives recurring security awareness training.
longText: Awareness and training controls in CJIS 6.0 require that personnel understand their responsibilities when handling CJI. Training must cover topics like proper use, social engineering, insider threats, and reporting incidents. Track who has completed training, ensure new hires are trained before being granted access, and refresh content at least every two years or when major policy changes occur.

title: Deliver Role-Based Training
shortText: Tailor CJIS training to the specific roles people perform, not just generic content.
longText: CJIS distinguishes between general awareness and role-based training. Administrators, security officers, dispatchers, and records personnel all interact with CJI differently and face different risks. Provide targeted training that covers the controls, procedures, and tools relevant to each role so they understand how CJIS policy applies to their daily work.

title: Log Important Security Events
shortText: Make sure CJIS systems log key events like logons, failures, and access to sensitive data.
longText: Audit and accountability controls require that CJIS systems generate records of security-relevant events. At a minimum, log successful and failed logons, privilege changes, configuration changes, and access to particularly sensitive CJI. Confirm that clocks are synchronized so timestamps are reliable, and ensure that logs are retained for the period defined by state or agency policy.
section: AU-2 Event Logging / AU-8 Time Stamps / AU-11 Audit Record Retention

title: Review Audit Logs on a Schedule
shortText: Don’t just collect logs—review and analyze them on a regular schedule.
longText: CJIS 6.0 expects agencies to actively use their audit logs to detect misuse and unusual patterns. Define how often each system’s logs are reviewed, who is responsible, and what constitutes suspicious activity. Document log reviews and follow-up actions so you can show auditors that monitoring is happening consistently, not just in response to incidents.
section: AU-6 Audit Record Review, Analysis, and Reporting

title: Protect Audit Logs from Tampering
shortText: Store CJIS audit logs so that only authorized staff can modify or delete them.
longText: Audit information must be protected from unauthorized access and modification. Store logs in secured repositories with access restricted to a small set of administrators or security personnel, and avoid giving application owners the ability to erase their own records. Consider write-once storage or centralized log management to improve integrity and support investigations.

title: Maintain and Test a Contingency Plan
shortText: Document how you will restore CJIS services after an outage—and test that plan.
longText: Contingency planning controls require agencies to prepare for disruptions such as hardware failure, cyber incidents, or facility loss. Create a written contingency plan that identifies critical CJIS systems, recovery priorities, roles, and procedures. Test the plan through exercises or simulations and update it based on lessons learned.
section: CP-2 Contingency Plan / CP-4 Contingency Plan Testing

title: Back Up CJIS Systems and Data Securely
shortText: Back up CJIS systems regularly and protect those backups with strong controls.
longText: CJIS requires that agencies be able to restore CJIS systems and data after a failure or incident. Perform backups on a schedule appropriate to the system’s criticality, protect backups with access controls and encryption, and periodically test restore procedures. Treat backup media as containing CJI and handle it accordingly, including secure storage and sanitization when retired.
section: CP-9 System Backup / CP-10 System Recovery and Reconstitution

title: Categorize Systems Handling CJI
shortText: Formally categorize systems based on the type and sensitivity of CJI they process.
longText: Risk assessment controls call for agencies to understand which systems process CJI and how critical they are. By categorizing systems, you can prioritize security investments, vulnerability scanning, logging, and monitoring where it matters most. Document the categorization and review it when systems change or new services are added.
section: RA-2 Security Categorization / RA-3 Risk Assessment

title: Scan for Vulnerabilities Routinely
shortText: Perform regular vulnerability scans on CJIS systems and act on the findings.
longText: CJIS 6.0 emphasizes vulnerability monitoring as part of a risk-based security program. Use approved tools to scan CJIS servers, workstations, and applications for known weaknesses. Prioritize remediation based on severity and exposure, and track remediation progress. Include authenticated scans where feasible so configuration issues are detected.

title: Control Changes to CJIS Systems
shortText: Use a formal change management process for all significant CJIS system changes.
longText: Configuration management controls require that agencies evaluate and document the impact of changes to CJIS systems before implementation. This includes software updates, new configurations, and infrastructure changes. Changes should be tested, approved by appropriate personnel, and tracked so you can reconstruct what was changed and why if problems arise.
section: CM-3 Configuration Change Control / CM-4 Impact Analyses

title: Limit Installed Software and Services
shortText: Remove unnecessary software, services, and features from CJIS systems.
longText: Least functionality is a key configuration principle in CJIS: systems should only run the components needed to support the mission. Extra services and applications increase the attack surface and make patching harder. Review installed software and enabled services on CJIS servers and workstations, remove what is not required, and restrict who can install new software.
section: CM-7 Least Functionality / CM-10 Software Usage Restrictions

title: Control Physical Access to CJIS Areas
shortText: Protect rooms and spaces that house CJIS systems with appropriate physical controls.
longText: Physical and environmental protection requirements include restricting who can enter areas where CJIS systems or media are located. Use locks, badges, visitor logs, and where appropriate, surveillance to control and record access. Ensure staff know that propping open secure doors or bypassing controls is not acceptable, even for convenience.
section: PE-2 Physical Access Authorizations / PE-3 Physical Access Control

title: Secure CJIS Media in Storage and Transit
shortText: Store and transport drives, discs, and printed CJI using secure methods.
longText: Media protection controls cover how CJI is handled on physical media, including backup tapes, USB drives, laptops, and printed reports. When not in use, store such media in locked containers or rooms with controlled access. When transporting, use tamper-evident packaging or locked cases, and maintain chain-of-custody where appropriate.
section: MP-4 Media Storage / MP-5 Media Transport

title: Sanitize Media Before Reuse or Disposal
shortText: Wipe or destroy any media that contained CJI before it is reused or discarded.
longText: CJIS 6.0 requires agencies to sanitize media so that CJI cannot be recovered once it is no longer needed. This may include secure erase procedures, degaussing where applicable, or physical destruction such as shredding or crushing. Keep records of media sanitization for audit purposes, especially when devices are sent to surplus or recycling.

title: Encrypt CJI in Transit
shortText: Use strong, FIPS-validated encryption when CJI crosses untrusted networks.
longText: Transmission confidentiality and integrity controls require that CJI sent over public or otherwise untrusted networks be protected from eavesdropping and tampering. Use VPNs, TLS, or other approved cryptographic mechanisms that rely on FIPS-validated modules. Periodically review cipher suites and protocol versions to ensure they meet current CJIS expectations.
section: SC-8 Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity / SC-13 Cryptographic Protection

title: Protect CJI at Rest with Encryption Where Appropriate
shortText: Use disk or database encryption for CJIS systems, especially on mobile and high-risk assets.
longText: CJIS recognizes the value of cryptographic protection for data at rest, particularly for laptops, mobile devices, and servers in less controlled environments. Full-disk encryption, secure key management, and strict access controls help prevent exposure if a device is lost or stolen. Evaluate which systems pose the highest risk and ensure they use approved encryption solutions.
section: SC-28 Protection of Information at Rest

title: Respond Quickly to Security Incidents
shortText: Have a documented process for detecting, reporting, and handling CJIS-related security incidents.
longText: Incident response controls in CJIS 6.0 require agencies to prepare for events such as suspected misuse, malware infections, or unauthorized access to CJI. Define how incidents are reported, who is notified, and what steps are taken to contain and remediate them. Practice the process with tabletop exercises and update it based on real-world experience.
section: IR-4 Incident Handling / IR-6 Incident Reporting

title: Use Approved Devices Only
shortText: Never access CJI from personal or unauthorized devices.
longText: CJIS requires agencies to restrict access to Criminal Justice Information (CJI) only to authorized, agency-managed devices. Personal laptops, tablets, or phones lack mandated security controls, logging, and encryption. Always use your assigned workstation or mobile device that meets CJIS technical and policy requirements.
section: 5.13 Policy Area 13 – Mobile Devices / 5.10 Policy Area 10 – System Configuration

title: Report Lost or Stolen Devices Immediately
shortText: If a CJI-capable device goes missing, report it right away.
longText: Missing devices present a direct threat to CJI confidentiality. Agencies must have procedures to rapidly revoke certificates, disable accounts, and wipe devices. Reporting promptly helps prevent unauthorized access and satisfies CJIS incident notification requirements.

title: Verify Email Senders Carefully
shortText: Always inspect suspicious emails before clicking.
longText: Phishing remains the #1 attack against law enforcement systems. Confirm sender identity, avoid downloading unexpected attachments, and never click outside login links. Report suspicious messages so they can be blocked for the entire agency.
section: 5.5 Policy Area 5 – Access Control / 5.3 Incident Response

title: Use Strong, Complex Passwords
shortText: Avoid simple or reused passwords—ever.
longText: CJIS Password Requirements mandate minimum complexity and resistance to guessing attacks. Never reuse passwords across systems. Create strong, unique passphrases and store them only in approved, secure, agency-sanctioned managers if permitted.

title: Beware Shoulder Surfing
shortText: Protect CJI from people looking over your shoulder.
longText: Criminal justice environments often include visitors, contractors, and non-sworn personnel. Always angle screens away from public view and lock your device when stepping away. Screen privacy filters may be required depending on area controls.

title: Use Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Every Time
shortText: Never bypass or delay MFA prompts.
longText: MFA is required for any remote or privileged access to CJI systems. It stops the majority of credential-theft attacks. If your MFA device malfunctions, report it rather than attempting workarounds.

title: Do Not Store CJI in Personal Notes
shortText: Never write CJI into notebooks, unapproved apps, or documents.
longText: CJI must only be stored in secure, authorized systems that meet CJIS security controls. Personal notebooks, unencrypted note apps, personal cloud storage, and messaging apps are prohibited locations for CJI storage.

title: Be Cautious When Printing
shortText: Only print CJI when absolutely necessary.
longText: Printed CJI must be physically secured, tracked, and disposed of properly. Use only authorized printers located in controlled areas. Retrieve printed pages immediately and never leave paperwork unattended.

title: Use CJIS-Approved Encryption
shortText: Encrypt CJI at rest and in transit with approved methods.
longText: CJIS Security Policy mandates advanced encryption (FIPS 140-2 or 140-3 validated) for all CJI transmitted over public networks or stored on mobile devices. Never email or transfer CJI unencrypted.

title: Keep Work Areas Clear
shortText: Never leave CJI visible on desks or walls.
longText: A clean-desk policy supports CJIS requirements for physical protection and media handling. Secure all printed documents, removable media, and notes containing sensitive information when not in active use.

title: Avoid Plugging in Unknown USB Drives
shortText: Do not connect unapproved USB or storage devices.
longText: Removable media is a major malware infection vector. Only agency-issued, encrypted USB drives are permitted for CJI handling. Unknown devices should be surrendered to IT for inspection.

title: Verify Identity Before Sharing Information
shortText: Ensure the requester is authorized before providing CJI.
longText: CJIS access is limited to personnel with a demonstrated need-to-know. Always authenticate callers or requesters using agency procedures. Do not rely solely on caller ID or email names.

title: Follow Proper Disposal Procedures
shortText: Never throw CJI in the trash—follow secure destruction rules.
longText: CJI must be destroyed using methods such as shredding, pulping, burning, or approved digital sanitization. Disposal must be documented when required. Improper disposal is a CJIS violation.

title: Avoid Public Wi-Fi
shortText: Never access CJI systems on public or guest networks.
longText: Public Wi-Fi lacks required encryption and can expose traffic to interception. CJIS requires secure network boundaries and protected communication channels. Always use agency networks or approved VPN.

title: Use Agency-Approved VPN Only
shortText: Do not use consumer VPNs or remote-desktop tools.
longText: CJIS-compliant remote access requires agency-controlled VPN, auditing, and MFA. Consumer VPNs, remote desktop apps, or screen-sharing services bypass mandated safeguards and must not be used.

title: Log Out When Finished
shortText: Always sign out of CJIS systems—not just close the window.
longText: Closing the app or browser does not always end active sessions. Logging out ensures session tokens cannot be reused and supports CJIS auditing requirements.

title: Use Agency Email Only for CJI
shortText: Never send CJI to or from personal email accounts.
longText: Personal email lacks CJIS-compliant controls, logging, and encryption. Only agency-managed email systems may be used for transmitting CJI, and only when secured as required.

title: Do Not Share Screenshots of CJI
shortText: Never capture or forward screenshots of sensitive data.
longText: Screenshots containing CJI are treated as digital media and must follow strict handling, storage, and disposal rules. Sharing them via messaging, cloud apps, or personal devices violates CJIS policy.

title: Secure Your Mobile Device
shortText: Use passcodes, biometrics, and lock settings.
longText: CJIS requires mobile devices accessing CJI to enforce passcodes, screen lock timers, encryption, and remote wipe capabilities. Keep these controls active at all times.

title: Be Alert to Social Engineering
shortText: Attackers may impersonate staff to gain access.
longText: Never provide credentials, reset codes, or sensitive information just because someone claims to be a coworker or technician. Validate identity using official channels before assisting.

title: Do Not Use Personal Cloud Storage
shortText: Never upload documents with CJI to personal cloud accounts.
longText: Services like iCloud, Google Drive, Dropbox, or OneDrive (personal accounts) are not CJIS-compliant. CJI must only be stored on approved, agency-managed systems with encryption, auditing, and access controls that meet CJIS requirements.

title: Check for HTTPS Before Entering Credentials
shortText: Always confirm you’re on a secure site before logging in.
longText: CJIS systems accessed through a browser must use encrypted HTTPS/TLS connections. If a site shows as 'Not Secure,' do not log in or continue. Report suspicious pages immediately.

title: Avoid Using Public Charging Ports
shortText: Public USB charging stations are a security risk.
longText: Attackers can install malware or extract data via compromised charging stations ('juice jacking'). Always use your own charger and a trusted power outlet when working with CJI-capable devices.

title: Secure Printed CJI in Locked Storage
shortText: Always lock up printed or physical CJI.
longText: CJIS requires physical protection of all media. Store printed CJI in locked cabinets, secure rooms, or other controlled spaces. Do not leave case files, printouts, or notes laying out in shared areas.

title: Review Your Access Annually
shortText: Ensure your permissions still match your duties.
longText: Agencies must conduct periodic access reviews to verify that users retain only necessary permissions. If your job role or responsibilities change, access should be updated immediately to maintain the principle of least privilege.

title: Use Only Agency-Approved Messaging Apps
shortText: Never send CJI through texting or consumer chat apps.
longText: Apps like SMS, iMessage, WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, Signal, and others are not approved for CJI transmission unless specifically authorized by the agency and configured per CJIS Security Policy.

title: Be Cautious With Shared Workspaces
shortText: Avoid viewing or discussing CJI around unauthorized personnel.
longText: CJIS requires strict control over who may see or overhear CJI. Avoid discussing sensitive details in hallways, public spaces, or mixed offices. Only handle CJI in controlled areas.

title: Use Screen Privacy Filters When Required
shortText: A privacy screen helps prevent shoulder-surfing.
longText: Depending on the workspace, CJIS may require privacy filters for mobile devices or laptops to prevent unauthorized people from viewing CJI over your shoulder.

title: Avoid Installing Unapproved Software
shortText: Do not download or install apps unless authorized.
longText: Unauthorized apps can compromise system integrity or bypass CJIS-mandated controls. Only IT-approved and agency-configured applications may be installed on CJI-capable devices.

title: Check URLs for Spoofing
shortText: Attackers often mimic official CJIS system websites.
longText: Fake login pages are used to steal credentials. Always double-check spelling, domain names, and certificate information. Report suspicious websites immediately.

title: Use Agency-Approved Remote Access Only
shortText: Never use personal remote-desktop tools.
longText: Tools like TeamViewer, Chrome Remote Desktop, or AnyDesk bypass logging and encryption requirements. Only use the agency’s CJIS-approved VPN, MFA, and remote-access solution.

title: Label CJI Properly
shortText: Documents containing CJI must be clearly marked.
longText: CJIS requires agencies to identify sensitive information. Mark printed or digital files that contain CJI to ensure proper handling, storage, and destruction.

title: Avoid Using Shared Accounts
shortText: Each person must use their own login.
longText: Shared accounts violate CJIS accountability rules. Every user must be uniquely identifiable so actions can be audited, investigated, and attributed.

title: Review Audit Logs When Required
shortText: Auditing helps detect suspicious activity.
longText: CJIS mandates auditing of access, authentication, viewing, modification, and transmission of CJI. If your role includes reviewing logs, do so diligently—it is often the first step in detecting misuse.

title: Maintain Updated Contact Info
shortText: Ensure your contact info is current for notifications.
longText: Agencies must be able to reach users quickly during security events. Keep your phone number, email, and MFA recovery methods up-to-date to avoid access interruptions.

title: Never Share MFA Tokens or Codes
shortText: Treat MFA codes like passwords.
longText: Attackers frequently use social engineering to obtain MFA codes. Never share them with anyone—not coworkers, supervisors, or IT staff. Valid support teams will never ask for them.

title: Protect CJI During Transport
shortText: Secure documents when traveling between facilities.
longText: CJI must be transported in sealed containers or secure cases. Do not leave files in cars, visible in public, or accessible during travel. Follow agency procedures for transfer and accountability.

title: Use Only Secure Disposal Bins
shortText: Shred CJI using approved shred bins or devices.
longText: Agencies often use locked shred bins or scheduled destruction services to dispose of CJI. Never use regular trash cans or recycling bins for any documents containing CJI.

title: Log Suspicious Activity Immediately
shortText: If something feels off, report it.
longText: CJIS requires immediate notification of possible breaches or suspicious access attempts. Whether you notice unusual behavior, system errors, or unknown login prompts—notify your security team.

title: Follow Password Change Requirements
shortText: Change your password when required by policy.
longText: CJIS requires timely password changes when compromise is suspected or mandated by policy. Use strong, unique passwords and update them as soon as the system instructs you.

title: Do Not Disable Antivirus or Endpoint Protection
shortText: Keep security tools enabled at all times.
longText: CJIS requires active malware protection on all systems that handle CJI. Never disable antivirus, EDR, or monitoring agents. If something interrupts your work, contact IT—do NOT turn protections off.

title: Report Lost or Stolen Devices Immediately
shortText: Lost devices mean potential CJI exposure.
longText: If a phone, tablet, laptop, or removable media that may contain or access CJI is lost, stolen, or misplaced, always report it immediately so remote lock/wipe and incident response can begin.

title: Use Only Agency-Approved Wireless Networks
shortText: Never access CJI over public Wi-Fi.
longText: CJIS prohibits connecting to unknown or unencrypted Wi-Fi networks when accessing or transmitting CJI. Always use agency-approved networks with encryption and proper authentication.

title: Log Off Shared or Public Computers
shortText: Always sign out when leaving a shared device.
longText: If you must use a shared terminal, log out completely when finished. Leaving an active session open violates auditing and accountability requirements and can expose CJI to unauthorized users.

title: Avoid Printing CJI Unless Necessary
shortText: Print only what you absolutely must.
longText: Printed CJI increases both storage and disposal risk. Only print when operationally necessary, retrieve printouts immediately, and secure them in accordance with physical protection requirements.

title: Verify Identity Before Sharing Information
shortText: Confirm who you're speaking with before discussing CJI.
longText: Impersonation attacks are increasingly common. Always confirm the identity of coworkers, IT staff, or partner agencies before sharing sensitive information or granting access.

title: Avoid Using Personal Devices for CJI
shortText: Use only agency-managed devices.
longText: Personal devices typically lack CJIS-required controls such as auditing, mobile device management, approved encryption, and configuration baselines. Only use officially managed equipment for CJI.

title: Encrypt Data in Transit
shortText: Always use secure encrypted channels.
longText: CJIS mandates encryption when transmitting CJI outside of a physically secure location. Ensure all email, file transfer, and remote access uses TLS or other approved encryption.

title: Do Not Bypass Web Filters
shortText: Security filters are required protections.
longText: Browser filters block dangerous or non-compliant sites. Attempting to circumvent them violates CJIS policy and increases exposure to malware, phishing, and data exfiltration.

title: Confirm Partner Agencies Are CJIS-Compliant
shortText: Only share CJI with authorized compliant partners.
longText: Before sending or receiving CJI with another agency, ensure they meet CJIS security requirements. Unauthorized sharing violates federal standards and can result in penalties.

title: Maintain Updated Software
shortText: Keep systems patched to reduce vulnerabilities.
longText: CJIS requires timely installation of security patches and system updates. Outdated software is a major vector for attacks. Never delay updates unless directed by IT.

title: Use Strong, Complex Passwords
shortText: Passwords must be resistant to guessing.
longText: Avoid dictionary words, personal information, or simple patterns. Follow CJIS requirements for minimum length, complexity, and lockout settings. Use passphrases whenever permitted.

title: Be Cautious With Unknown USB Devices
shortText: Never plug in random or unknown USB drives.
longText: Attackers often use USB devices to deliver malware or steal data. Only use agency-issued and approved removable media. Immediately report any suspicious device.

title: Physically Secure Your Badge
shortText: Treat your access badge like a key.
longText: Your badge grants physical access to secure areas where CJI may be stored or processed. Never lend it out or leave it unattended. Report lost or stolen badges immediately.

title: Avoid Copying CJI to Notes Apps
shortText: Do not store sensitive info in personal notes.
longText: Notes apps (Apple Notes, Samsung Notes, Notepad, Google Keep, etc.) are not approved CJI storage locations. Only use authorized systems that meet CJIS encryption and auditing requirements.

title: Close Browser Tabs After Use
shortText: Don’t leave CJIS portals open in a browser.
longText: Leaving tabs open risks unauthorized viewing, session hijacking, or cached data exposure. Always log out and close browser windows after accessing CJI systems.

title: Protect Stored Photos and Screenshots
shortText: Never store CJI in your camera roll.
longText: Photos containing CJI must not be saved on personal or unmanaged devices. Even agency devices must follow strict storage, deletion, and encryption requirements.

title: Use Secure Conference Tools
shortText: Only use approved platforms for virtual meetings involving CJI.
longText: Zoom, Teams, or similar tools must be agency-configured to meet CJIS requirements. Never discuss CJI using personal or unapproved meeting tools.

title: Do Not Leave Terminals Unattended
shortText: Stay with your device when CJI is accessible.
longText: Unattended devices expose CJI to unauthorized individuals and violate CJIS physical protection rules. Lock your screen whenever stepping away—even briefly.

title: Respect Sensitive Compartments Within Systems
shortText: Only access case files that you are authorized to view.
longText: CJIS requires agencies to enforce role-based compartmentalization of data. Accessing information beyond your need-to-know violates both CJIS and agency policy.

title: Never Store CJI in Email Drafts
shortText: Draft folders are not secure CJI storage.
longText: Email draft folders often sync to multiple devices and cloud storage locations. CJIS prohibits storing CJI in unencrypted or non-audited systems, including drafts. Type sensitive information only when you are prepared to send it through an approved encrypted method.

title: Always Use Agency-Approved VPN
shortText: Access CJI systems only through secure remote connections.
longText: When working remotely, CJIS requires the use of encrypted, agency-managed VPN connections with FIPS-validated cryptography. Public, home, or cellular connections alone are not sufficient without the VPN in place.

title: Avoid Using Browser Autofill for Credentials
shortText: Do not save CJIS credentials in browsers.
longText: Browser password managers store credentials locally and often sync across devices, which may violate CJIS authentication and auditing requirements. Always enter CJIS credentials manually or use approved password management systems.

title: Use Bookmarks for CJIS Portals
shortText: Avoid mistyping URLs that could lead to phishing sites.
longText: Manually typing URLs increases the risk of landing on look-alike phishing pages. Bookmark official CJIS systems and agency portals to ensure you always access trusted, verified URLs.

title: Secure Radios and Dispatch Consoles
shortText: Treat radio access as sensitive equipment.
longText: Radio consoles and dispatch terminals often provide access to operational CJI. Ensure they are locked when not in use, follow authentication procedures, and prevent unauthorized personnel from viewing or hearing sensitive traffic.

title: Shred All CJI Printouts
shortText: Use cross-cut shredders for disposal.
longText: CJIS requires secure disposal of CJI. Printed materials must be destroyed using approved cross-cut shredders or secure destruction services. Never throw CJI printouts into normal trash or recycling.

title: Log Out of Mapping and CAD Systems
shortText: CAD and RMS contain sensitive data too.
longText: Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD) and Records Management Systems (RMS) store CJI and must be protected. Log out when not actively using them and ensure your screen is locked during breaks.

title: Validate Fax Numbers Before Sending CJI
shortText: Double-check fax recipients for accuracy.
longText: Sending CJI to an incorrect fax number is a reportable breach. Always verify numbers via official directories and confirm that receiving devices are in secure, controlled environments.

title: Wear Visitor Badges in Secure Areas
shortText: Visitors require proper identification.
longText: Anyone who is not fully CJIS-certified must be properly identified and escorted. Visitor badges help staff quickly identify who needs an escort and who is not authorized to access certain areas.

title: Don’t Use AI Tools for CJI
shortText: Never input real CJI into AI or cloud assistants.
longText: Most public AI platforms do not meet CJIS requirements for data handling, auditing, storage, and encryption. Do not upload or paste any CJI into AI tools unless the system has been formally approved.

title: Report Suspicious Emails Immediately
shortText: Phishing is one of the top threats to CJI.
longText: Unsolicited, unexpected, or unusual emails may be attempts to compromise CJIS systems. Do not open attachments or click links. Immediately notify your security or IT team.

title: Check for HTTPS Before Logging In
shortText: Encrypted websites protect your credentials.
longText: Before entering credentials into any portal containing CJI, ensure the site uses HTTPS with a valid certificate. A missing padlock icon is a red flag for phishing or insecure configurations.

title: Physically Lock Rooms Containing CJI
shortText: Rooms with CJI systems require controlled access.
longText: CJIS requires that areas with CJI—servers, dispatch terminals, RMS stations—remain locked or continuously monitored. Never leave secure areas unattended or propped open.

title: Store Mobile Devices Securely
shortText: Keep agency phones and tablets locked away.
longText: When not in use, mobile devices with access to CJI must be secured in locked drawers, cabinets, or rooms. This prevents theft and accidental exposure.

title: Never Email CJI to Personal Accounts
shortText: Personal email is not CJIS-compliant.
longText: Forwarding CJI to Gmail, Yahoo, Outlook.com, or any personal email service violates CJIS encryption, auditing, and storage requirements. Only use agency-approved messaging systems.

title: Do Not Use Personal Cloud Storage
shortText: Never store CJI in iCloud, Dropbox, Google Drive, etc.
longText: Personal cloud services lack mandatory CJIS controls like FIPS-validated encryption, auditing, and controlled access. Use only agency-approved secure storage solutions.

title: Follow Agency Backup Procedures
shortText: Backups must meet CJIS security standards.
longText: Only authorized IT staff should manage backups of systems containing CJI. Backups must be encrypted, physically protected, and tested regularly to ensure recoverability.

title: Be Cautious With Portable Hard Drives
shortText: External drives must be encrypted.
longText: If portable drives are required for legitimate operational needs, they must use FIPS 140-2 validated encryption. Unencrypted drives can result in unauthorized disclosure if lost or stolen.

title: Use Secure Messaging for Dispatch Traffic
shortText: Texting CJI must be done through approved apps only.
longText: Dispatch messages containing CJI must be sent only through agency-approved, encrypted messaging platforms. Personal SMS, iMessage, or other consumer apps are not permitted.

title: Review Access Lists Regularly
shortText: Make sure only authorized personnel retain access.
longText: CJIS requires periodic review of user access rights. Staff who change roles, retire, or transfer should have access removed immediately. Regular audits ensure compliance and reduce risk.

title: Do Not Leave CJI Visible in Vehicles
shortText: Never store printed CJI in your car.
longText: CJIS prohibits leaving paper CJI or mobile devices containing CJI unattended in vehicles, even if doors are locked. Cars are not secure facilities and can be easily broken into. Always bring materials with you or return them to a secure area.

title: Beware of Tailgating Into Secure Areas
shortText: Ensure people badge in individually.
longText: Unauthorized individuals may attempt to follow employees into secured spaces without badging. Politely ask unknown persons to badge in or contact security. Physical access control is a required CJIS safeguard.

title: Use Approved Removable Media Only
shortText: Do not use personal USB drives for CJI.
longText: Removable media must meet strict CJIS encryption, auditing, and protection standards. Only use agency-issued, encrypted media for storing or transferring CJI. Personal or unidentified drives pose major security risks.

title: Keep Server Rooms Locked
shortText: Access to infrastructure must be restricted.
longText: Areas containing switches, servers, CJIS routers, or authentication equipment must remain locked and monitored. Only authorized personnel should enter, and access should be logged according to policy.

title: Verify Faxed CJI Was Received Securely
shortText: Follow up on important fax transmissions.
longText: After sending CJI via fax, confirm that the intended authorized recipient received it and that the fax machine is located in a secure location. Misrouted faxes are a common breach source.

title: Lock Mobile Devices Immediately
shortText: Do not leave agency phones unlocked.
longText: Smartphones and tablets must lock automatically after a short period. If you step away, lock the device manually. Mobile devices often contain cached or synced CJI that must be protected at all times.

title: Avoid Discussing CJI in Public
shortText: Conversations can be overheard easily.
longText: Restaurants, hallways, elevators, and public areas are not appropriate places to discuss cases or sensitive criminal justice details. Keep all CJI conversations within controlled environments.

title: Secure CJIS Login Tokens
shortText: Treat authentication tokens like passwords.
longText: Hardware tokens, smartcards, and authentication keys must remain in your possession at all times. Do not lend them to coworkers or leave them behind on desks or in vehicles.

title: Do Not Reuse Personal Passwords
shortText: CJIS credentials must be unique.
longText: Using the same password for CJIS systems and personal accounts increases risk of credential theft. Follow agency requirements to maintain unique, strong, and secure passwords.

title: Follow Chain-of-Custody Procedures for Digital Media
shortText: Evidence and CJI must be tracked at all times.
longText: When handling digital media containing CJI or evidence, document transfers, storage locations, and custodians. Proper chain-of-custody prevents legal and security issues.

title: Do Not Install Browser Extensions
shortText: Extensions can leak or collect sensitive data.
longText: Many browser extensions have broad permissions that can read keystrokes, capture screen content, or monitor browsing. Install extensions only if explicitly approved by IT and necessary for your role.

title: Use Two-Person Integrity When Required
shortText: Some CJIS tasks require dual authorization.
longText: Certain sensitive operations—such as system reconfiguration or data destruction—may require two authorized personnel present. Follow agency procedures to maintain integrity and accountability.

title: Never Leave Keys in Secure Areas
shortText: Protect keys to evidence rooms and secure storage.
longText: Physical keys controlling access to CJI must be closely protected. Leaving keys unattended can lead to unauthorized entry and compromise of sensitive data or evidence.

title: Beware of Fake Tech Support Calls
shortText: Attackers impersonate IT personnel.
longText: If someone claiming to be IT requests remote access, passwords, or MFA codes, verify their identity through official channels. Real IT staff will not ask for sensitive info through unsolicited contact.

title: Use Approved Methods for File Transfers
shortText: CJI cannot be sent through unapproved file-sharing services.
longText: Tools like personal Dropbox, Google Drive, and consumer file-transfer apps are not CJIS-compliant. Use only agency-approved encrypted file transfer systems or secure-sharing platforms.

title: Follow Access Revocation Procedures
shortText: Access must be removed when no longer needed.
longText: CJIS requires timely revocation of access for personnel who retire, transfer, or change duties. Immediately report role changes so access rights can be updated to maintain compliance.

title: Do Not Prop Open Secure Doors
shortText: Always keep secure areas properly closed.
longText: Propping open controlled-access doors defeats security controls designed to protect CJI. Even during loading or unloading, maintain physical access controls.

title: Avoid Using Third-Party Camera Apps
shortText: Only approved camera applications may capture CJI.
longText: Third-party camera apps may sync photos automatically to cloud services or lack proper encryption. Use only agency-approved devices and applications when capturing images containing CJI.

title: Follow Retention Schedules for Media
shortText: CJI must be retained only as long as required.
longText: Each agency has defined retention periods for documents, recordings, and evidence. Holding CJI longer than necessary increases risk and violates CJIS guidelines.

title: Do Not Leave Case Files on Conference Tables
shortText: Clear rooms of CJI after meetings.
longText: Whether using printed reports, photos, or digital devices, all CJI must be secured after meetings. Never leave sensitive material behind in common areas or conference rooms.

title: Keep Devices Updated Automatically
shortText: Enable automatic updates when allowed.
longText: CJIS requires timely installation of security patches. Automatic updates help ensure devices remain secure. Never postpone updates indefinitely; if an update prevents work, notify IT rather than disabling update services.

title: Use Only Approved Bluetooth Devices
shortText: Bluetooth accessories must be authorized.
longText: Unapproved Bluetooth accessories—keyboards, headsets, or speakers—may transmit data insecurely. Only use agency-approved Bluetooth devices when accessing or handling CJI.

title: Be Aware of People Watching During Logins
shortText: Shield your screen when entering passwords.
longText: Shoulder surfing is a simple and effective way attackers steal credentials. Always position screens to reduce visibility and cover your keyboard or device while typing sensitive information.

title: Keep Network Ports Covered
shortText: Unused ports must be secured or disabled.
longText: Open Ethernet or network ports can allow unauthorized devices to connect to secure networks. Notify IT if you find unlocked or exposed ports in secure areas.

title: Report System Glitches or Strange Behavior
shortText: Even small errors can indicate compromise.
longText: Unusual pop-ups, slowdowns, or login failures may signal malware or unauthorized access. Report anomalies immediately so IT can investigate—do not ignore them.

title: Secure Whiteboards with Sensitive Information
shortText: Erase or cover whiteboards after discussions.
longText: Whiteboards used for case discussions, diagrams, or sensitive notes must be erased before leaving the room. Pictures of whiteboards containing CJI must never be taken unless approved.

title: Do Not Leave Laptops Charging in Public Areas
shortText: Unattended charging laptops can expose CJI.
longText: Laptops with access to CJI—even if locked—must not be left charging or sitting unattended in public spaces. Always keep them within view or secure them in a locked area.

title: Use Only Approved Browsers
shortText: Not all browsers meet CJIS requirements.
longText: Some browsers lack the security controls needed for CJIS compliance. Use only the browsers explicitly approved by your agency to ensure encryption, auditing, and security settings are enforced.

title: Always Identify Visitors in CJIS Areas
shortText: Challenge unknown people politely.
longText: If you see someone in a controlled area without proper credentials or escort, verify their identity. CJIS requires that unauthorized personnel be denied access to areas where CJI is stored or viewed.

title: Avoid Storing CJI Temporarily on Desktops
shortText: Do not save CJI files to your desktop or downloads.
longText: Local folders like 'Desktop' or 'Downloads' lack proper encryption and often sync to cloud backups. Save CJI only in authorized, encrypted agency storage locations.

title: Avoid Reusing USB Devices Across Systems
shortText: Cross-system use increases contamination risks.
longText: USB drives used on personal, home, or non-agency systems may carry malware. Never connect external devices used on untrusted systems to CJIS-connected devices.

title: Follow Laptop Travel Procedures
shortText: CJI-capable laptops require secure transport.
longText: When transporting laptops that can access or store CJI, use lockable cases and keep the device with you at all times. Never place them in checked luggage or leave them unattended in vehicles.

title: Check for Security Seals on Media
shortText: Tamper-evident seals protect CJI transfers.
longText: When using physical media for evidence or CJI transfer, ensure tamper-evident seals are intact. Report any broken or missing seals to a supervisor immediately.

title: Never Screenshot CJI from System Interfaces
shortText: Screenshots create untracked sensitive media.
longText: Screenshots automatically save to local or cloud storage and often bypass audit controls. Avoid screenshots entirely unless explicitly allowed by formal policy and stored securely.

title: Report Unauthorized Physical Access Attempts
shortText: Even harmless attempts must be documented.
longText: If someone attempts to enter a secure area without proper authorization—intentionally or accidentally—report it immediately so the incident can be logged and investigated.

title: Secure Your Workspace at the End of Each Day
shortText: A clean workspace protects sensitive material.
longText: At the end of your shift, remove or secure any printed documents, notes, removable media, or devices. Daily cleanup supports CJIS physical protection and media safeguarding requirements.

title: Lock Your Computer Automatically
shortText: Enable automatic screen locking.
longText: CJIS requires automatic locking of workstations after a period of inactivity. Never disable this feature. If your system does not lock automatically, report it to IT immediately.

title: Avoid Using Public Printers or Copiers
shortText: Only use approved devices for printing CJI.
longText: Public printers and non-agency copiers may store image data and are not protected by CJIS-required security controls. Only print CJI using authorized agency-managed printers.

title: Keep Mobile Hotspots Secure
shortText: Hotspots must use strong passwords and encryption.
longText: If authorized to use a mobile hotspot, ensure the SSID is hidden when possible, uses WPA3 or WPA2 encryption, and has a strong unique password. Unauthorized users must never connect.

title: Verify Caller Authorization Before Sharing CJI
shortText: Phone-based social engineering is increasing.
longText: Before sharing CJI over the phone, ensure the caller is authenticated using agency protocols. Caller ID is unreliable and easily spoofed. Always follow need-to-know validation steps.

title: Avoid Using Forgotten or Old Accounts
shortText: Legacy accounts pose security risks.
longText: Old or unused accounts often lack strong monitoring and may not meet current CJIS policy requirements. If you encounter unused accounts, report them so they can be reviewed or disabled.

title: Do Not Share Device Unlock Codes
shortText: Keep PINs and passcodes private.
longText: Device unlock patterns, PINs, and passcodes must not be shared. CJIS requires that authentication methods remain under the sole control of the authorized user.

title: Check for Security Patches Before Using New Software
shortText: Ensure new applications are fully updated.
longText: Even agency-approved software must be patched to eliminate vulnerabilities. Before first use, verify that all required updates are installed and automatic updates are enabled.

title: Use Strong Device Encryption
shortText: Encryption protects lost or stolen devices.
longText: CJIS requires FIPS 140-2 validated encryption for devices containing or accessing CJI. Never disable encryption, and report any device that appears unencrypted.

title: Maintain Secure Work Habits in Remote Locations
shortText: Even at home, CJI must stay protected.
longText: Do not leave CJI-visible screens exposed to family members, guests, or roommates. Secure your workspace and follow all physical and digital safeguards.

title: Never Upload CJI to Non-Agency Email Apps
shortText: Avoid third-party email clients.
longText: Personal or unapproved email apps may sync messages to cloud servers outside CJIS compliance. Only send or receive CJI through authorized, agency-managed email applications.

title: Limit Information Shared in Voicemail
shortText: Voicemail is not a secure channel.
longText: Never leave CJI or case details in voicemail messages. If needed, leave a callback number and request the recipient return the call through official channels.

title: Be Careful with Auto-Print or Auto-Sync Settings
shortText: Disable automatic forwarding or syncing.
longText: Automatic print queues, folder syncs, or auto-forward rules may accidentally expose CJI. Check your settings regularly and disable any that aren’t authorized.

title: Avoid Using Split-Screen for CJI
shortText: Keep CJI windows isolated.
longText: Using split-screen features with non-secure apps may lead to accidental screen exposure. Keep CJI applications full-screen or in dedicated secure workspaces.

title: Do Not Allow Others to 'Borrow' Your Login Session
shortText: Every user must authenticate individually.
longText: Allowing others to work under your logged-in session violates auditing, access control, and accountability requirements. Everyone must use their own credentials.

title: Validate Network Connections Regularly
shortText: Ensure you're connected to the correct Wi-Fi.
longText: Attackers may create look-alike SSIDs to trick users. Always verify you're connected to an approved, agency-managed network before accessing CJIS systems.

title: Securely Remove Unauthorized Apps from Devices
shortText: Only approved applications are allowed.
longText: Personal apps can store data, track activity, or sync information. Remove any unapproved app from CJIS-used devices and notify IT if you are unsure.

title: Position Screens Away from Public View
shortText: Avoid accidental disclosure.
longText: Screens displaying CJI must be positioned to prevent unauthorized individuals from viewing sensitive information. Use privacy screens when necessary.

title: Do Not Use Quick-Share Features
shortText: Disable AirDrop and similar tools.
longText: AirDrop, Nearby Share, and similar tools may accidentally share files with unintended recipients. Disable such features unless explicitly approved and configured securely.

title: Review Logs for Assigned Systems
shortText: Auditing supports CJIS compliance.
longText: If your role includes system oversight, regularly review audit logs for suspicious access or anomalies. Reporting unusual entries helps protect systems from compromise.

title: Ensure Mobile Device Backups Are Encrypted
shortText: Backups must meet encryption standards.
longText: Mobile device backups must use encrypted, agency-approved methods. Personal cloud backups are not allowed and may leak sensitive information.

title: Do Not Use Third-Party Keyboards
shortText: Keyboard apps can capture keystrokes.
longText: Third-party keyboard apps may record input or sync typed data to external servers. Use only default or agency-approved keyboards for devices accessing CJI.

title: Always Verify File Attachments Before Opening
shortText: Unknown attachments often contain malware.
longText: Do not open attachments unless you trust the sender and expected the file. Even internal-looking messages may be spoofed. When unsure, report it to IT.

title: Secure Portable Evidence Recorders
shortText: Body cameras and recorders contain sensitive data.
longText: Store body-worn cameras, audio recorders, and digital evidence devices in secure locations. Follow upload and storage procedures carefully to maintain CJIS compliance.

title: Avoid Using Public Charging Stations
shortText: Public USB charging can lead to data theft.
longText: Attackers may modify public charging stations to exfiltrate data. Charge CJIS-capable devices only using approved chargers or agency-owned power equipment.

title: Use Only Approved Remote Access Tools
shortText: Unauthorized remote tools violate CJIS.
longText: Do not use remote desktop programs like TeamViewer, AnyDesk, or personal screen-sharing apps to access CJIS systems. Only agency-approved remote access platforms with encryption and auditing are authorized.

title: Avoid Storing CJI in Browser Downloads
shortText: Browser download folders are not secure.
longText: Files saved to browser download folders may sync to cloud services or remain unencrypted. Save CJI only to authorized, protected storage locations specified by your agency.

title: Use Privacy Screens on Laptops
shortText: Blocks unauthorized viewing.
longText: Privacy screen filters help reduce the chance of someone observing CJI on your screen, especially in shared spaces. They reinforce CJIS physical protection requirements.

title: Keep Audio Devices Secure
shortText: Headsets and mics may capture CJI.
longText: Do not leave headsets, microphones, or conference equipment plugged in or unattended in secure areas. These devices can accidentally capture or leak sensitive information.

title: Avoid Personal Hotspots Unless Authorized
shortText: Only use secure network options.
longText: Personal hotspots often lack enterprise-grade encryption and monitoring. Use them only if explicitly authorized and configured according to CJIS guidelines.

title: Follow Multi-Factor Authentication Rules
shortText: MFA must be used whenever required.
longText: CJIS requires MFA for remote access and certain privileged operations. Do not disable MFA prompts, store MFA codes, or approve login requests you did not initiate.

title: Keep CJIS Documents Away From Cameras
shortText: Cameras can unintentionally record sensitive data.
longText: Avoid placing printed reports or screens containing CJI in view of surveillance cameras, webcams, or video conferencing systems unless explicitly approved.

title: Minimize Use of Personal Notes
shortText: Handwritten notes may contain CJI.
longText: If you must take physical notes related to CJI, secure them like any other sensitive record. Do not store CJI on sticky notes, notebooks, or unprotected paper.

title: Keep Printers in Secure Locations
shortText: CJI output must be protected.
longText: Printers that may output CJI must be located in controlled areas where unauthorized individuals cannot view or retrieve printed documents.

title: Check for Locked Filing Cabinets
shortText: Physical documents must be secured.
longText: Any filing cabinet or binder containing CJI must be locked when not in use. If a cabinet is left unlocked, secure it immediately and notify a supervisor if needed.

title: Do Not Use Social Media for Work Communication
shortText: Social platforms are not CJIS-compliant.
longText: Never share case details, operational plans, or CJI through Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, WhatsApp, or any social platform. These tools lack required CJIS protections.

title: Be Cautious When Working Outdoors
shortText: Screens may be visible to bystanders.
longText: If working outside (crime scenes, vehicles, field locations), ensure screens displaying CJI are shielded from public view. Use screen privacy or position yourself strategically.

title: Do Not Store CJI on Shared Drives Without Controls
shortText: Shared folders must enforce access restrictions.
longText: Only place CJI in shared drives that enforce permissions, logging, and encryption. Public or open-access directories are prohibited for CJI storage.

title: Disable Personal Voice Assistants
shortText: Siri, Alexa, and others may record audio.
longText: Voice assistants can activate unintentionally and record sensitive conversations. Disable them on any device used near CJI unless specifically approved by policy.

title: Follow Secure Disposal for Digital Media
shortText: Old drives must be wiped properly.
longText: Digital media containing CJI must be destroyed or sanitized using approved methods such as degaussing, shredding, or certified wipe tools. Normal deletion is insufficient.

title: Avoid Leaving CJI Visible on Display Boards
shortText: Erase boards promptly after meetings.
longText: Dry-erase boards, magnetic boards, and case boards must be cleared after use. Photos of boards containing CJI are restricted unless approved as part of evidence handling.

title: Use Secure Methods for Password Recovery
shortText: Avoid insecure recovery practices.
longText: Do not store password hints or recovery answers in insecure places. CJIS requires secure handling of authentication information, especially for privileged accounts.

title: Never Leave Evidence Uploads Unattended
shortText: Stay present while files are transferring.
longText: When uploading CJI or evidence to secure systems, remain at the workstation until the upload finishes to prevent tampering or accidental exposure.

title: Do Not Mix Personal and Work Tabs
shortText: Separate CJIS systems from personal browsing.
longText: Personal tabs, social media, or entertainment sites should not be open while accessing CJI. Cross-site scripts and tracking tools may capture data inadvertently.

title: Use Secure Approved Methods for Audio Transcription
shortText: Transcription tools must meet CJIS standards.
longText: AI transcription tools, mobile apps, and online services may upload audio to third-party servers. Only use agency-approved CJIS-compliant transcription solutions for interviews or recordings.

title: Do Not Use Personal Email to Reset Work Passwords
shortText: Password recovery must stay within CJIS controls.
longText: Using personal email for account recovery violates CJIS authentication requirements. Recovery steps must occur only through agency-managed, secure systems to prevent unauthorized access.

title: Avoid Leaving Sensitive Tabs Open During Calls
shortText: Video calls may capture your screen.
longText: During video meetings, ensure that any tabs or applications displaying CJI are closed. Accidental screen shares are a common cause of unintentional data exposure.

title: Secure Your Desk Before Visitors Enter
shortText: Hide sensitive materials before meetings.
longText: When hosting visitors—technicians, auditors, community members—ensure all CJI, printed materials, or open systems are secured or covered. Visitors must not view CJI.

title: Use Agency-Approved Messaging for Team Communication
shortText: Do not use personal messaging apps.
longText: Apps like iMessage, SMS, WhatsApp, and Facebook Messenger do not meet CJIS standards for encryption and auditing. Only use agency-approved messaging platforms for operational discussions.

title: Disconnect From Networks When Not Needed
shortText: Limit exposure time.
longText: If you do not need to be connected to CJIS systems or wireless networks, disconnect when appropriate. This reduces attack surface and prevents unauthorized background access.

title: Avoid Using Free Online PDF Tools
shortText: Document converters may upload files externally.
longText: Free PDF merge or convert tools often upload files to unknown servers. CJI must never be processed using unapproved third-party online tools.

title: Label CJI Clearly When Required
shortText: Follow agency marking guidelines.
longText: Although CJIS does not mandate classification markings, your agency may require labels such as 'CJI—Sensitive.' Proper labeling reduces accidental exposure and improves handling.

title: Never Accept Unknown Bluetooth Pairing Requests
shortText: Unauthorized devices may attempt to connect.
longText: Attackers may use Bluetooth pairing to access devices. Decline all unsolicited pairing prompts immediately and notify IT if they persist.

title: Always Use Locked Storage For Evidence Drives
shortText: Portable drives with CJI must remain secured.
longText: Store evidence drives, forensic media, and CJI USB devices in locked, controlled-access storage. Even brief exposure can lead to unauthorized disclosure.

title: Avoid Using Auto-Login Features
shortText: Credentials must not auto-fill into CJIS systems.
longText: Auto-login features bypass required authentication safeguards. CJIS requires all access to be tied to authenticated, auditable user sessions.

title: Report Stolen Credentials Immediately
shortText: Even suspected theft must be reported.
longText: If you suspect someone has obtained your password, token, or PIN, report it immediately. Delayed reporting significantly increases breach risk and violates policy.

title: Do Not Use Public Cloud Backups
shortText: Backups must meet CJIS encryption standards.
longText: Personal cloud services like iCloud Backup, Google One, or OneDrive cannot store CJI unless specifically configured and approved by the agency.

title: Secure Video Footage Containing CJI
shortText: Handle surveillance content properly.
longText: Security footage or bodycam videos often contain CJI. Only store, transfer, or share these files using approved CJIS-compliant systems and channels.

title: Use Proper Procedures for Remote Wipe
shortText: Lost devices require quick action.
longText: If an agency device is lost or stolen, IT may need to initiate a remote wipe. Do not attempt to wipe devices yourself without guidance; incorrect handling may affect investigations.

title: Do Not Store Evidence Photos in Personal Albums
shortText: Camera roll storage violates CJIS.
longText: Evidence photos must be captured and uploaded using agency-approved secure apps. Personal photo galleries are not CJIS-compliant and may sync to cloud accounts.

title: Use Strong Protection for Encryption Keys
shortText: Keys must be handled securely.
longText: Encryption keys protect CJI. Do not store keys in email, shared files, or unsecured notes. Follow agency procedures for key handling and rotation.

title: Do Not Leave Chat Sessions with CJI Visible
shortText: Chats often contain sensitive case information.
longText: If you step away, close or minimize messaging apps that contain incident details or CJI to prevent unauthorized viewing.

title: Use Agency-Approved Screen Capture Tools When Authorized
shortText: Only capture CJI when necessary and approved.
longText: If your role requires capturing screens containing CJI, use only approved and logged tools. CJIS requires traceability and proper handling of captured data.

title: Follow Procedures for External Agency Requests
shortText: Verify authorization before releasing CJI.
longText: External agencies must have appropriate agreements and authorization to receive CJI. Always verify MOUs, user agreements, or legal requirements before sending sensitive information.

title: Use Secure Calls for Sensitive Conversations
shortText: Avoid discussing CJI on personal phones.
longText: Personal cell phones and commercial VoIP systems may route calls through insecure networks. Use agency-approved, encrypted voice systems for sensitive discussions whenever possible.

title: Avoid Copying CJI Into Spreadsheets Unless Approved
shortText: Spreadsheets can leak sensitive information.
longText: Many spreadsheet apps auto-save to cloud storage or sync between devices. Only enter CJI into spreadsheets if the file location, device, and application are all approved by your agency and meet CJIS standards.

title: Lock Evidence Rooms at All Times
shortText: Never leave evidence storage areas unsecured.
longText: Evidence rooms often contain CJI and sensitive media. Even brief periods unlocked can result in unauthorized access or chain-of-custody issues. Ensure doors remain locked whenever unattended.

title: Be Cautious When Forwarding Email Threads
shortText: Hidden CJI may be buried in the conversation.
longText: Email chains often contain sensitive data from earlier messages. Review the entire thread before forwarding to ensure you're not sharing CJI with unauthorized recipients.

title: Do Not Store Passwords in Plaintext Files
shortText: Avoid storing credentials in notes or documents.
longText: Passwords stored in plaintext—Notes apps, text files, email drafts—violate CJIS password-handling requirements. Use only agency-approved password management solutions.

title: Turn Off Device Screens When Talking to Visitors
shortText: Prevent shoulder-surfing of CJI.
longText: If someone enters your workspace who is not authorized to see CJI, lock your device or put the display to sleep. Even a quick glance can expose sensitive information.

title: Check Email Recipients Carefully Before Sending
shortText: Autofill mistakes are a common breach source.
longText: Always double-check the 'To' and 'CC' fields when sending messages involving sensitive data. Email autofill often selects unintended recipients, risking unauthorized disclosure.

title: Ensure Remote Sessions Time Out Properly
shortText: Idle sessions must close automatically.
longText: CJIS requires automatic timeout for remote sessions. If your remote access does not log out automatically after inactivity, report it so proper safeguards can be configured.

title: Avoid Writing Case Information on Sticky Notes
shortText: Temporary notes can become permanent risks.
longText: Sticky notes are easy to lose and often left in open areas. Never record CJI or passwords on sticky notes. Use approved secure digital or physical storage methods instead.

title: Use Dedicated Work Accounts Only
shortText: CJIS work must stay on CJIS accounts.
longText: CJIS-related work cannot be conducted using personal accounts or shared accounts. Every action must be traceable to an individual user under auditing requirements.

title: Protect File Metadata
shortText: Metadata can reveal sensitive information.
longText: File names, timestamps, and embedded metadata may contain CJI. Before transmitting or storing files, ensure metadata does not reveal case identifiers, locations, or other sensitive info.

title: Use Proper Handling for Subpoenaed CJI
shortText: Legal requests must follow procedure.
longText: When responding to subpoenas or legal requests involving CJI, follow agency guidance and ensure data is transferred using CJIS-compliant methods only. Unauthorized disclosures may violate federal regulations.

title: Do Not Leave Maps or Tactical Plans Visible
shortText: CJI includes operational information.
longText: Maps, tactical sheets, patrol routes, and operational plans must not be left on desktops or walls in unsecured areas. These materials reveal sensitive operational patterns.

title: Use Locked Containers for Transporting Documents
shortText: Transporting CJI requires physical protection.
longText: Documents containing CJI must be transported in sealed, locked containers or envelopes. Do not carry exposed papers through public areas.

title: Never Assume an Email Attachment is Safe
shortText: Even known senders may be compromised.
longText: Malware often arrives disguised as a familiar contact. Verify any unexpected attachment before opening it, and report anything suspicious to IT immediately.

title: Secure Network Diagrams and System Maps
shortText: These diagrams contain sensitive infrastructure details.
longText: Network architecture diagrams, IP ranges, and server maps should be stored and shared only through CJIS-compliant systems. Exposure can enable targeted cyberattacks.

title: Avoid Discussing CJI Over Speakerphone
shortText: Use private voice channels when possible.
longText: Speakerphones can expose CJI to anyone nearby. Use handset mode or approved headsets to limit who can hear sensitive case information.

title: Always Verify Device Certificates
shortText: Trusted certificates ensure secure connections.
longText: If you see certificate warnings when accessing CJIS portals, stop immediately. Invalid or mismatched certificates may indicate a man-in-the-middle attack.

title: Don’t Leave Digital Forensic Images Unsecured
shortText: Forensic data is extremely sensitive.
longText: Forensic images often contain complete device snapshots including CJI, personal data, and evidence. Store them only on approved encrypted drives or forensic servers.

title: Review Automated Alerts Promptly
shortText: Alerts may indicate attempted breaches.
longText: System alerts about failed logins, suspicious network connections, or unauthorized access attempts must be reviewed quickly. Rapid response supports CJIS incident handling requirements.

title: Avoid Using Pages with Auto-Sync Capability
shortText: Some apps sync content to the cloud by default.
longText: Apps like Apple Notes, OneNote, Evernote, and Google Docs automatically sync content to external servers. Unless your agency has approved the configuration, do not store CJI in these apps.

title: Protect Documents During Transport Between Buildings
shortText: Walking between facilities still requires secure handling.
longText: When carrying printed CJI between agency buildings, ensure papers are sealed in envelopes or folders that conceal sensitive content. Even short walks can expose information to unauthorized individuals.

title: Disable Auto-Save to Personal Cloud Accounts
shortText: Ensure documents save only to approved locations.
longText: Some apps automatically back up documents to personal cloud accounts. CJIS prohibits this. Confirm that auto-save is disabled or configured to use secure, agency-managed storage.

title: Verify Secure Deletion of Temporary Files
shortText: Temp files may contain hidden CJI.
longText: Applications often store temporary versions of documents. Confirm that temporary files created by CJIS-related work are properly deleted or stored only in secure, approved locations.

title: Avoid Using Email For Large CJI Transfers
shortText: Large files should be shared securely.
longText: Email systems may fail to encrypt large attachments end-to-end. Use agency-approved secure file transfer platforms or portals instead of email for high-volume CJI files.

title: Always Lock Devices Before Plugging Into Projectors
shortText: Prevent accidental projection of CJI.
longText: If you connect a laptop to a projector or conference system, lock your screen first. Ensure only non-sensitive visuals are displayed before unlocking.

title: Verify Encryption Before Uploading to Evidence Systems
shortText: Not all upload methods are equally secure.
longText: Before uploading digital evidence or CJI to online systems, confirm that the platform enforces encryption in transit and at rest. Report any outdated or unencrypted interfaces.

title: Avoid Logging In Through Untrusted Networks
shortText: Networks like hotels or coffee shops are unsafe.
longText: Even with a VPN, untrusted public networks increase the risk of interception or rogue access points. Connect only through approved networks or secure mobile hotspots.

title: Check For Unauthorized Video Recording Devices
shortText: Cameras must not capture CJI unintentionally.
longText: Smart displays, webcams, and conference cameras may record when not intended. Ensure recording devices are off or covered when working with CJI unless approved and logged.

title: Keep Backup Drives in Secure Environments
shortText: Backup media requires the same protection as primary data.
longText: Backup drives containing CJI must be secured with physical locks, encryption, and strict access controls. Do not leave backups on desks or unsecured shelves.

title: Do Not Upload CJI to Electronic Whiteboards
shortText: Smart boards may store or sync content.
longText: Interactive whiteboards, touchscreen displays, and smart meeting devices often keep copies of displayed materials. Avoid sharing CJI with such systems unless configured and approved.

title: Follow Proper Disposal Procedures for Labels and Stickers
shortText: Labels may contain sensitive identifiers.
longText: Case numbers or personal identifiers on labels, stickers, or packaging must be shredded or destroyed securely—not thrown in the trash—since they may reveal CJI.

title: Check for Updated CJIS Security Addendums
shortText: Vendors must have current agreements.
longText: Before sharing CJI with contractors or service providers, confirm that their CJIS Security Addendum is current, signed, and compliant. Outdated agreements may violate federal requirements.

title: Keep System Logs Protected From Modification
shortText: Logs must remain tamper-proof.
longText: CJIS requires audit logs be protected from unauthorized access or modification. If you notice missing or altered log entries, report the issue immediately.

title: Use Secure Methods for Password Sharing in Emergencies
shortText: Emergency access must still follow protocol.
longText: Emergency access procedures must use approved break-glass accounts or official escalation steps—not personal messages, sticky notes, or handwritten passwords.

title: Avoid Posting CJI on Internal Social Platforms
shortText: Internal chat boards may still lack protections.
longText: Even internal discussion boards or department-wide chat systems may not meet CJIS encryption or auditing standards. Share CJI only in officially approved systems.

title: Ensure Time-Based Logouts Are Not Disabled
shortText: Timeout settings must remain active.
longText: Automatic logout reduces unauthorized access risk. Do not alter or bypass timeout controls, even for convenience. If they malfunction, notify IT immediately.

title: Be Careful When Mirroring Displays
shortText: Mirroring may show CJI on unintended screens.
longText: If your device can mirror to TVs, conference screens, or wireless displays, ensure mirroring is turned off when working with CJI. Unauthorized display can cause accidental exposure.

title: Correctly Classify Call Recordings
shortText: Recorded CJI must be handled securely.
longText: Call recordings involving CJI must be stored in approved evidence systems with encryption and audit controls. Do not keep recordings on personal devices or unapproved servers.

title: Follow Proper Remote Session Closure Procedures
shortText: Always close remote sessions fully.
longText: Closing only the window of a remote session may not log you out. Ensure you fully sign out and terminate connections to prevent unauthorized access.

title: Review Permissions on Shared Mailboxes
shortText: Shared inboxes must have proper access restrictions.
longText: If your agency uses shared mailboxes, ensure only authorized individuals have access and that CJI is handled according to policy. Remove users promptly when roles change.

title: Avoid Leaving CJIS Portals Logged In During Breaks
shortText: Always log out when stepping away.
longText: Leaving CJIS portals open and unattended—even for a short break—violates physical protection rules. Always lock your screen or fully log out to prevent unauthorized access.

title: Use Agency-Approved Methods for Redaction
shortText: Improper redaction can expose sensitive data.
longText: Do not use basic drawing tools or black boxes for redaction. Use approved software that permanently removes underlying text or data, ensuring CJI cannot be recovered.

title: Ensure Secure Handling of Paper Intake Forms
shortText: Temporary documents must be treated as CJI.
longText: Paper forms containing names, DOBs, case info, or fingerprints must be stored securely until digitized or filed. Never leave intake forms on counters or shared tables.

title: Use Dedicated Workstations for CJI When Possible
shortText: Minimize crossover between personal and CJIS tasks.
longText: Workstations accessing CJI should not be used for non-work tasks like browsing, social media, or personal email. Segregated environments reduce exposure and malware risks.

title: Check for Hidden Data Before Sharing Documents
shortText: Metadata may contain CJI.
longText: Documents may include hidden properties such as edits, comments, tracked changes, or GPS data. Review and scrub metadata before sharing files externally.

title: Do Not Use Free USB Charging Cables
shortText: Cables may contain embedded malware.
longText: Attackers can hide malicious hardware inside USB cables. Only use agency-issued or trusted charging cables for devices that store or access CJI.

title: Follow Secure Handling for Multi-Function Printers
shortText: MFPs store data internally.
longText: Many printers, scanners, and copiers store cached images. Ensure your agency’s MFPs are configured securely and do not store CJI unnecessarily.

title: Use Secure File Naming Conventions
shortText: Avoid revealing case details in filenames.
longText: Filenames can appear in logs, browser history, or backups. Avoid using sensitive info such as case numbers, victim names, or addresses in filenames.

title: Ensure Tablets Are Logged Out After Field Use
shortText: Field devices must be secured promptly.
longText: When returning from field activities, close open files, log out of CJIS apps, and ensure devices sync only over secure agency networks.

title: Do Not Store CJI in Voice Notes or Dictation Apps
shortText: Voice apps may upload recordings.
