longText: Many mobile dictation apps send audio to cloud servers for processing. Use only agency-approved systems for recording or transcribing statements.

title: Use Secure Trash and Recycling Bins
shortText: Paper CJI must not enter normal waste streams.
longText: Shredded or unshredded CJI must never enter public trash or recycling bins. Use only agency-managed secure destruction containers.

title: Secure Case-Related Maps and Photos
shortText: Printed imagery may reveal sensitive locations.
longText: Documents that include crime scenes, target locations, or surveillance imagery must be stored in secure areas. Do not leave them in public workspaces or briefing rooms.

title: Turn Off Nearby Smart TVs and Displays
shortText: Smart devices may record or mirror screens.
longText: Some smart TVs, casting devices, or displays may capture or mirror content. Disable casting features and ensure displays are not passively recording while working with CJI.

title: Confirm Permissions Before Starting Data Transfers
shortText: Transfers must be authorized.
longText: Before sending CJI to another department or agency, ensure their access level and agreements are current. Unauthorized transfers may violate CJIS information exchange rules.

title: Disconnect External Drives After Use
shortText: Avoid leaving CJI-containing drives attached.
longText: External drives containing sensitive media must be removed and secured after use. Leaving them connected increases the risk of unauthorized access or malware spread.

title: Read Banner Warnings Before Logging In
shortText: Login banners contain legal requirements.
longText: Login banners often include consent-to-monitor notices and use restrictions mandated by CJIS. Reviewing them helps reinforce authorized-use expectations.

title: Avoid Printing CJI to Unsecured Remote Print Services
shortText: Remote printing services may store data externally.
longText: Services like cloud print, wireless hotel printers, or retail print centers cannot be used for CJI. Only agency-controlled printers meet required protections.

title: Use Proper Packaging for Mailed CJI
shortText: Mailing must follow strict guidelines.
longText: When mailing CJI, use double envelopes, tamper-evident seals, and trackable shipping methods. Never place CJI in standard outgoing mail bins.

title: Keep Password Reset Tokens Secure
shortText: Tokens must be protected like passwords.
longText: If you receive a password reset token, link, or one-time code, do not forward or share it—ever. Treat reset credentials as highly sensitive authentication material.

title: Check External Devices for Physical Tampering
shortText: Tampered hardware can steal data.
longText: Before using card readers, scanners, kiosks, or terminals, check for loose panels or unusual attachments. Tampered devices may intercept CJI or login data.

title: Avoid Using Browser Sync Features
shortText: Syncing may send data to non-CJIS cloud services.
longText: Browser sync features such as Chrome Sync or Firefox Sync may upload bookmarks, history, passwords, and cached data to cloud servers that are not CJIS compliant. Ensure sync is disabled on devices used for CJIS access.

title: Secure Dash Cameras and Vehicle Computers
shortText: In-car systems may store CJI.
longText: Patrol vehicle computers, dash cameras, and mobile terminals often contain CJI. Lock vehicles, secure devices, and ensure that dash-cam storage is encrypted and uploaded only through approved systems.

title: Use Strong Security Questions
shortText: Avoid easy-to-guess answers.
longText: Security questions should not include publicly known details such as birthdays, pets, or hometowns. CJIS requires strong authentication practices—choose answers that cannot be guessed or found online.

title: Do Not Leave Open Files Visible in Shared Offices
shortText: Close documents when others enter.
longText: If someone enters a shared workspace—contractor, maintenance staff, or another department—close CJIS files on your screen or cover printed material immediately.

title: Limit Use of Clipboard Copy/Paste
shortText: Clipboard data may persist unexpectedly.
longText: Some systems store clipboard content across apps or sync it with other devices. Avoid copying CJI into clipboard tools unless you confirm clipboard syncing is disabled.

title: Check Desks for Residual Impressions
shortText: Writing impressions may reveal sensitive data.
longText: Writing with pressure can leave indent marks on notepads or desk pads. After writing CJI on paper, ensure leftover indentations are not readable and store documents securely.

title: Avoid Using Hidden Folders to Store CJI
shortText: Hiding files is not securing them.
longText: Hidden folders or obscure filenames do not meet CJIS protections. CJI must be stored only in encrypted, access-controlled directories managed or approved by the agency.

title: Use Secure Methods When Discussing CJI Over Radio
shortText: Follow radio protocols carefully.
longText: Some radios may transmit unencrypted by default. Always follow agency procedures for what types of information may be shared over radio channels and avoid broadcasting sensitive identifiers unless required.

title: Secure Forensic Workstations
shortText: Forensic machines contain highly sensitive data.
longText: Digital forensic machines often contain full device images, logs, and CJI. Ensure they remain in physically restricted rooms, are locked when not in use, and accessed only by authorized personnel.

title: Properly Handle Returned IT Equipment
shortText: Devices must be sanitized before reassignment.
longText: When laptops, tablets, or phones are turned in, they must undergo CJIS-approved sanitization before being repurposed. Do not donate, recycle, or dispose of devices without IT clearance.

title: Verify Caller Identity for Password Reset Requests
shortText: Social engineering often targets help desks.
longText: If someone calls requesting a password reset or account assistance, verify their identity through approved channels. Attackers often impersonate employees to gain access.

title: Use Secure Methods for Sharing Case Updates
shortText: Do not text case details.
longText: Sending case updates, reports, or identifiers via personal text or group chats violates CJIS media handling requirements. Use secure, agency-approved messaging or records systems.

title: Avoid Leaving Audio Evidence Unattended
shortText: Audio files often contain sensitive interviews.
longText: Interview recordings, dispatch audio, and forensic sound files must be secured in approved locations. Do not leave them on desktops, conference tables, or shared drives.

title: Ensure Printers Automatically Clear Memory
shortText: Printer memory may retain CJI.
longText: Some printers store document images in internal memory. Confirm that printers handling CJI erase cached data or are configured to restrict unauthorized access.

title: Avoid Reading Case Documents in Public Places
shortText: Bystanders may see your screen or papers.
longText: Even if sitting in a quiet coffee shop or waiting room, avoid reviewing digital or printed CJI. Public environments are never appropriate for CJI access.

title: Use Secure Methods for Internal Transfers
shortText: Internal email is not always encrypted.
longText: Not all internal email systems meet CJIS encryption requirements by default. Ensure internal transfers of CJI use encrypted channels or secure file transfer options as required.

title: Monitor Badge Access Reports
shortText: Unusual badge activity may indicate compromise.
longText: If your role includes physical security oversight, review badge logs for unusual access patterns such as after-hours entry or repeated access failures.

title: Double-Check Recipients When Using Secure Portals
shortText: Mistakes can still happen in secure systems.
longText: Even in encrypted, authorized portals, CJI must be sent only to recipients with a verified need-to-know. Always confirm recipient lists before submitting.

title: Use Agency-Issued Cables and Accessories
shortText: Unapproved accessories can compromise devices.
longText: Cheap or unknown cables, adapters, and dongles may include malicious firmware. Use only agency-approved accessories to connect or charge devices used for CJIS access.

title: Follow Cancellation Procedures for Lost Access Cards
shortText: Lost cards must be disabled immediately.
longText: If your access badge is lost or stolen, report it immediately so physical access can be revoked. CJIS requires strict control over secure area access points.

title: Avoid Leaving Case Files Visible During Screen Sharing
shortText: Close sensitive apps before sharing your screen.
longText: When presenting or sharing your screen in meetings, ensure all windows containing CJI are fully closed—not just minimized. Accidental exposure during screen sharing is a frequent cause of data leakage.

title: Follow Device Replacement Procedures Carefully
shortText: CJI devices must be wiped before replacement.
longText: When replacing a laptop, phone, or tablet that accesses CJI, follow agency procedures to ensure secure backup, wipe, and handoff. Do not simply trade in or discard these devices.

title: Use Approved Software for Scanning Documents
shortText: Unapproved apps may upload scans to cloud servers.
longText: Scanning apps on mobile devices or unapproved scanners often sync files automatically to cloud storage. Only use agency-approved scanners or secure mobile scanning solutions.

title: Check for Auto-Connect Network Settings
shortText: Disable auto-join for unknown Wi-Fi networks.
longText: Auto-connect features may join unsecured or rogue networks without your awareness. Disable auto-join for all networks except those explicitly authorized by the agency.

title: Confirm CJIS Compliance Before Using Dashboards
shortText: Analytic dashboards must be secured.
longText: Business intelligence tools, analytics dashboards, and case-tracking platforms must use encrypted, access-controlled connections. Do not load CJI into non-approved dashboards.

title: Avoid Using Wireless Keyboards or Mice Unless Approved
shortText: Wireless peripherals may be insecure.
longText: Wireless keyboards and mice can be intercepted or spoofed. Use only agency-approved models with encrypted communication channels.

title: Properly Log Out of Shared Kiosks or Dispatch Systems
shortText: Shared systems require extra caution.
longText: Dispatch consoles, lab kiosks, or other shared devices must be fully logged out after use. Do not assume the next person will secure your session.

title: Check Case Numbers Before Sending Documents
shortText: Ensure you’re sending the right file.
longText: Before emailing, printing, or uploading case files, verify the case number. A common error is sending documents from a similarly numbered but unrelated case.

title: Avoid Using Predictable File Passwords
shortText: Weak file passwords put CJI at risk.
longText: If password protection is used for documents or archives, ensure passwords meet CJIS complexity requirements. Avoid using case numbers, agency names, or predictable patterns.

title: Ensure Screens Lock During Automatic Sleep
shortText: Sleep mode must require authentication.
longText: Devices should require reauthentication after waking from sleep or standby mode. Report any system that wakes without a password or biometric login.

title: Avoid Referencing Victim or Witness Names in Nonsecure Apps
shortText: Use initials or secure channels when needed.
longText: If you must communicate operational information outside of CJIS systems, remove personally identifiable details. However, never reduce CJI security—use secure platforms whenever possible.

title: Secure Email Archives and PST Files
shortText: Exported email files must be encrypted.
longText: If emails are archived into PST, MBOX, or EML formats, they must be stored only in encrypted, CJIS-approved locations. These files often contain large amounts of sensitive data.

title: Handle Inter-Agency USB Media With Care
shortText: Shared media must follow chain-of-custody rules.
longText: If USB drives or other media are exchanged between agencies, ensure each transfer is documented, encrypted, and securely transported. Never hand off media informally.

title: Verify Secure Wi-Fi Certificates
shortText: Untrusted certificates may indicate rogue networks.
longText: When connecting to agency Wi-Fi, ensure the certificate shows the correct issuing authority. If a certificate warning appears, disconnect immediately and report the issue.

title: Disable File Sharing When Working With CJI
shortText: Network sharing may expose sensitive files.
longText: Ensure system-wide file sharing, AirDrop, SMB shares, or peer-to-peer sharing features are disabled unless explicitly required for an approved workflow.

title: Ensure Access Badges Are Not Duplicated
shortText: Badge cloning is a security threat.
longText: Some badges can be cloned if left unattended or scanned by malicious devices. Keep badges shielded and report any suspicious behavior near badge readers.

title: Limit Use of Browser Plugins
shortText: Plugins may log keystrokes or track data.
longText: Only install browser plugins approved by your agency. Many plugins collect browsing data, track keystrokes, or sync information externally—violating CJIS requirements.

title: Avoid Using Personal Smart Devices Near CJI
shortText: Smart devices constantly collect data.
longText: Smart speakers, home assistants, smartwatches, and IoT devices may listen, record, or transmit data. Keep them away from areas where CJI is discussed or viewed.

title: Check That Email Auto-Forward Rules Are Disabled
shortText: Auto-forwarding may send email externally.
longText: Unauthorized auto-forward rules can leak CJI to personal email accounts or external addresses. Review and disable auto-forwarding unless expressly approved.

title: Avoid Keeping CJI in Browser History
shortText: History may expose URLs or case details.
longText: Some case management or RMS systems include identifiers in their URLs or page titles. Ensure browsing sessions are cleared regularly and that CJIS sessions are not cached.

title: Be Careful When Using Email Search
shortText: Search results may reveal CJI unexpectedly.
longText: Searching your email inbox may show message previews containing CJI. Ensure no unauthorized individuals can see your screen while you are searching or reviewing message histories.

title: Avoid Storing CJI in Clipboard Managers
shortText: Some systems retain clipboard history.
longText: Clipboard managers can store multiple copied items and sync them between devices. Disable clipboard history and avoid copying CJI unless necessary for specific secure workflows.

title: Secure Case Folders During Transport in the Field
shortText: Fieldwork requires extra caution.
longText: Printed case folders used in patrol cars or field work must be stored securely when not in use. Keep them locked in compartments or in secured bags according to agency policy.

title: Check the Area for Sensitive Documents Before Meetings
shortText: Shared rooms can expose CJI.
longText: Before holding a meeting in a shared space, check the table, whiteboards, and projector screens to ensure no leftover documents or CJI materials from previous users remain visible.

title: Use Proper Storage for Interview Notes
shortText: Handwritten notes can contain CJI.
longText: Interview notes must be stored in secure evidence lockers or approved filing systems. Avoid leaving them in vehicles, backpacks, or personal bags after interviews.

title: Verify Email Encryption Before Sending
shortText: Some systems show encryption indicators.
longText: Check your email client for visual indicators confirming encryption is active. If encryption is not available, use a different approved method for sending the information.

title: Monitor Who Is Behind You When Handling CJI
shortText: Be aware of your surroundings.
longText: Shoulder surfing occurs even inside agency buildings. When working with CJI, ensure no unauthorized personnel are standing behind or near you.

title: Avoid Storing CJI in Email 'Sent Items'
shortText: Sent folders may retain sensitive attachments.
longText: After sending encrypted CJI, ensure sent emails are stored according to agency retention rules. Some agencies require periodic deletion or archiving within secure systems.

title: Use Secure Methods for Delivering Court Documents
shortText: Court filings may include CJI.
longText: When delivering documents to courts or prosecutors, use sealed envelopes and avoid handing documents to unauthorized personnel. Use documented receipt processes when required.

title: Secure Tablets During Multi-Agency Operations
shortText: Operational environments increase exposure risk.
longText: During joint operations or large field events, ensure tablets accessing CJIS systems are tethered, secured, or kept in supervised areas. Do not leave devices unattended at staging sites.

title: Do Not Store Backup Authentication Codes on Devices
shortText: Backup codes must be handled securely.
longText: MFA backup codes stored on mobile phones, laptops, or notes apps can compromise account security. Store them only in approved secure locations or password managers.

title: Follow CJIS Rules for Handling Juvenile Records
shortText: Juvenile CJI requires heightened caution.
longText: Juvenile records often require specific handling, storage, and disclosure restrictions mandated by state and federal law. Always follow agency policy for working with juvenile CJI.

title: Secure Printed Photos Immediately After Use
shortText: Photos may display sensitive scenes.
longText: Printed photographs used for briefings or identification must be returned to secure storage after use. Do not leave them on desks, bulletin boards, or briefing tables.

title: Do Not Use Portable Wi-Fi Repeaters
shortText: Unauthorized repeaters can expose networks.
longText: Portable repeaters or boosters may route traffic through insecure channels. Use only agency-installed or agency-approved network infrastructure.

title: Secure Email Distribution Lists
shortText: Ensure distribution lists are up-to-date.
longText: Email lists used for sending CJI must exclude unauthorized personnel. Review and update distribution lists periodically to prevent accidental disclosure.

title: Avoid Using Smart Pens or Digital Note Devices
shortText: Some devices sync notes to external servers.
longText: Smart pens and digital notebooks may upload handwritten notes to cloud accounts. Do not use them for interviews or CJI-related documentation unless approved.

title: Check Encrypted Drives for Proper Functioning
shortText: Encryption must be active and verified.
longText: If using encrypted portable drives, periodically verify encryption status and password protection. Drives failing to mount properly may indicate encryption issues.

title: Label Evidence Containers Clearly
shortText: Proper labeling prevents mishandling.
longText: Containers holding CJI or evidence must be labeled without exposing sensitive case details. Use case numbers or approved identifiers rather than names or incident specifics.

title: Avoid Using Public USB Adapters or Charging Stations
shortText: Juice jacking is a real threat.
longText: Public USB outlets may be modified to steal data or inject malware. Charge devices only using agency-approved chargers connected to secure power sources.

title: Ensure Deleted Files Are Removed From Cloud Trash
shortText: Trash folders may sync across devices.
longText: Deleting a file from a synced folder may still leave copies in cloud-based trash or revision history. Ensure deleted CJI files are fully purged according to agency procedures.

title: Avoid Leaving CJI Visible in Car Sun Visors or Door Pockets
shortText: Vehicles are not secure storage locations.
longText: Documents tucked into visors or door pockets can be seen through windows or fall out when doors open. Store CJI in locked compartments or secure bags while in transit.

title: Avoid Saving CJI to Desktop Folders
shortText: Desktop folders are often unencrypted.
longText: Files saved to the desktop may sync to cloud systems or be excluded from agency encryption policies. Store CJI only in approved secure directories.

title: Do Not Use Screenshot Tools for Sensitive Material
shortText: Screenshots may auto-sync to clouds.
longText: Many devices store screenshots in automatically synced cloud folders. Disable screenshot syncing or avoid capturing CJI with screenshots altogether.

title: Follow Proper Storage Requirements for Printed Mugshots
shortText: Mugshots are considered CJI.
longText: Mugshots printed for briefings or identification must be stored securely after use. Do not leave them unsecured in patrol cars, booking areas, or briefing rooms.

title: Ensure Device Auto-Updates Are Enabled
shortText: Security patches protect access to CJI.
longText: Devices accessing CJI must receive regular operating system and application updates. Ensure automatic updates are enabled to stay compliant with CJIS security requirements.

title: Verify Encryption Before Uploading Files to RMS
shortText: RMS uploads must occur over secure connections.
longText: Before uploading documents, photos, or reports to the Records Management System (RMS), ensure the connection is encrypted (HTTPS or VPN) and authorized by your agency.

title: Avoid Sharing Case Information Over VoIP Apps
shortText: Consumer VoIP apps may store call data.
longText: Consumer voice apps like WhatsApp, Skype, or FaceTime may log call metadata or recordings. Use agency-approved communication channels for discussions involving CJI.

title: Check Document Headers and Footers for CJI
shortText: Hidden identifiers may be exposed accidentally.
longText: Documents may contain headers or footers with case numbers, names, or timestamps that appear during printing or sharing. Always remove unneeded metadata.

title: Securely Store Body-Worn Camera Charging Docks
shortText: Docks may store cached video.
longText: Body-worn camera docks often contain internal memory buffers for uploaded footage. Keep docking stations in secure, access-controlled areas.

title: Avoid Leaving Whiteboards With Case Notes Un-erased
shortText: Whiteboards often expose sensitive case details.
longText: Erase whiteboards immediately after use, especially if they include names, addresses, case numbers, or investigative details. Photograph and store notes securely if needed.

title: Follow Proper Protocol for Sharing Criminal Histories
shortText: Criminal histories must follow strict rules.
longText: Criminal history information may only be shared with authorized individuals who have a verified need-to-know. Do not summarize or relay information informally.

title: Avoid Using Browser-Based PDF Editors
shortText: Online PDF editors may store uploaded files.
longText: Do not upload CJIS documents to websites that edit PDFs. Use approved offline PDF tools or agency-controlled document systems only.

title: Keep Conference Room Computers Logged Out
shortText: Shared spaces require strict control.
longText: Meeting-room computers or presentation PCs may be used by many employees. Always log out fully when finished to prevent unauthorized CJI access.

title: Avoid Leaving Digital Evidence on Personal Drives
shortText: Personal USB drives are rarely encrypted.
longText: Digital evidence or CJI must never be stored on personal flash drives, SD cards, or external storage. Use only agency-managed encrypted media.

title: Confirm Logging Is Enabled on CJIS Systems
shortText: Logs are essential for accountability.
longText: CJIS systems must generate audit logs for access attempts, changes, and deletions. If you discover logging is disabled or malfunctioning, report it immediately.

title: Turn Off Bluetooth When Not Required
shortText: Bluetooth vulnerabilities can expose devices.
longText: Bluetooth can be exploited to intercept communications or access devices. Disable Bluetooth when not in use, especially on devices accessing CJIS systems.

title: Use Agency-Managed Wi-Fi Hotspots in the Field
shortText: Avoid personal or consumer hotspots.
longText: Personal mobile hotspots or consumer MiFi devices may not meet CJIS encryption or logging standards. Use agency-issued, preconfigured hotspots only.

title: Ensure Visitor Logs Are Maintained in Secure Areas
shortText: Visitor tracking is mandatory.
longText: Any person entering a CJIS-sensitive area must be logged and escorted. Ensure visitor logs are accurate, complete, and stored securely for auditing.

title: Avoid Using Remote Desktop Tools Not Approved by the Agency
shortText: Unauthorized remote tools pose major risks.
longText: Remote desktop applications like TeamViewer or AnyDesk may bypass CJIS protections. Use only agency-approved remote access systems with logging and encryption.

title: Review System Permissions After Personnel Changes
shortText: Remove access promptly.
longText: When someone changes roles, retires, or leaves the agency, their CJIS access must be removed promptly. Regularly review privileges for accuracy and compliance.

title: Confirm Secure Wipe Before Reassigning Storage Media
shortText: Reused drives must be sanitized.
longText: Before hard drives, SSDs, or USB devices are reassigned within your agency, they must undergo a CJIS-approved sanitization process to ensure no CJI remains recoverable.

title: Use Approved Systems for Case Collaboration
shortText: Do not collaborate via personal cloud tools.
longText: Collaboration tools like Google Docs, Dropbox, or consumer Microsoft accounts may not be CJIS compliant. Use only agency-approved case collaboration platforms.

title: Cover Laptop Cameras in Sensitive Areas
shortText: Cameras can record unintentionally.
longText: Laptop webcams may activate through malware or software errors. Use agency-approved camera covers when working with CJI in sensitive locations.

title: Avoid Printing Complete Social Security Numbers
shortText: Use truncated formats whenever allowed.
longText: If a workflow allows for it, print only the last four digits of a Social Security number. This reduces exposure risk and aligns with CJIS privacy principles.

title: Verify Automatic Logoff Settings on Shared Systems
shortText: Shared devices must log out promptly.
longText: Shared terminals such as booking stations, dispatch consoles, or interview-room computers must auto-logout after a short inactivity period. Report systems that remain logged in indefinitely.

title: Avoid Using Browser Autofill for CJIS Credentials
shortText: Never save login credentials.
longText: Do not store CJIS usernames or passwords in browser autofill or password managers that are not agency-approved. Saved credentials can lead to unauthorized access.

title: Review Access Logs Regularly If You Are a Supervisor
shortText: Supervisors must verify compliance.
longText: If your role includes overseeing system access, review logs regularly to ensure only authorized personnel are accessing sensitive CJIS systems.

title: Store Unattended Devices in Secure Lockers
shortText: Lock devices when away from office.
longText: If you must leave a device at work overnight or during extended periods, store it in a secure, agency-provided locker or storage area. Do not leave devices unattended on desks.

title: Avoid Including Case Info in Calendar Events
shortText: Calendar notes may sync externally.
longText: Do not include CJI—names, case numbers, or event details—in calendar entries on devices that sync to the cloud. Use non-sensitive descriptions instead.

title: Verify That Old Evidence Files Are Removed From Local Storage
shortText: Keep evidence only where policy allows.
longText: Local file storage on desktops or laptops should not contain outdated evidence files. Ensure all materials are uploaded to proper systems and delete local copies securely.

title: Be Alert for Tailgating at Secure Doors
shortText: Authorized entry does not guarantee authorized access.
longText: Tailgating—following someone into a secure access point—is a common physical security breach. Ensure every person entering badges in separately.

title: Avoid Using Personal Contact Lists for Work Numbers
shortText: Contacts may sync to cloud accounts.
longText: Storing work-related contacts in personal phonebooks can sync data to non-CJIS cloud systems. Use agency-managed contact systems instead.

title: Always Use Multi-Factor Authentication Where Available
shortText: MFA is required on CJIS systems.
longText: If a CJIS system supports MFA, it must be enabled. MFA reduces the risk of unauthorized access due to compromised passwords.

title: Treat Near-Miss Incidents as Reportable
shortText: Close calls still matter.
longText: If you nearly sent CJI to the wrong recipient or temporarily left documents unsecured, report the incident. Near-misses help improve security programs and reduce future risk.

title: Avoid Leaving Devices Logged Into Mapping or Analytics Tools
shortText: Some tools expose sensitive data overlays.
longText: GIS systems, crime-mapping tools, or analytics dashboards may display CJI-linked overlays or datasets. Log out or close windows when leaving your workstation.

title: Check for Hidden Image Data in Digital Photos
shortText: EXIF data may contain location info.
longText: Before uploading or sharing photos containing CJI, verify that EXIF metadata such as GPS coordinates, timestamps, or device identifiers is removed when required.

title: Follow Proper Security for Temporary Staff
shortText: Contractors must meet CJIS requirements.
longText: Temporary staff, interns, or contractors must undergo background checks, sign Security Addendums, and receive CJIS training before accessing any CJI.

title: Confirm That Firewalls Block Unauthorized Traffic
shortText: System boundaries must be protected.
longText: Ensure firewall configurations block inbound and outbound connections not explicitly approved by your agency. Misconfigured firewalls expose CJIS systems to external threats.

title: Stay Up-To-Date With CJIS Security Training
shortText: Training is required annually.
longText: CJIS Security Awareness Training must be completed every year. Ensure you stay current on training requirements and complete them before expiration.

title: Always Store Devices Accessing CJI in Locked Areas Overnight
shortText: Even locked screens are not enough.
longText: CJIS requires devices that contain or access CJI to be stored in secure, locked environments after work hours. Locked screens alone do not meet physical security requirements.
